title: Lorentzian Polynomials and the Incidence Geometry of Tropical Linear Spaces
speaker: Jayden Wang
abstract: The theory of stable polynomials features a key notion called proper position, which generalizes interlacing of real roots to higher dimensions. I will show how a Lorentzian analog of proper position connects the structure of spaces of Lorentzian polynomials and the subtle incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces. The central object is the moduli space of codimension-1 tropical linear subspaces of a given tropical linear space. In one direction, the geometry of this moduli space can be detected using Lorentzian polynomials; conversely, by answering certain incidence problems, one discovers non-obvious convex cones of Lorentzian polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Two problems and the Lorentzian–tropical bridge
proof_stages.summary: The talk poses two linked questions—convexity phenomena for Lorentzian polynomials and incidence properties of tropical linear spaces—and explains that multiaffine Lorentzian polynomials, tropical linear spaces, and valuated matroids are connected by a round-trip dictionary.

proof_stages.label: Proper position and one-sided Lorentzian convexity
proof_stages.summary: After recalling stable-polynomial proper position and its upper/lower cone properties, the speaker defines Lorentzian proper position via adjoining one variable and emphasizes that only the upper cone property survives in general.

proof_stages.label: Valuated matroid quotients and incidence problems
proof_stages.summary: Tropical linear spaces are encoded by valuated matroids, containment is encoded by valuated matroid quotients, three classical incidence properties are formulated tropically, and elementary quotients are related to Lorentzian proper position of basis-generating polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Tropical convexity of codimension-one incidence spaces
proof_stages.summary: Tropicalizing the upper cone property, together with a matroid-duality argument, proves that the space DR_1(mu) of codimension-one tropical subspaces contained in a fixed tropical linear space is tropically convex.

proof_stages.label: Linearity and quotient-lattice structure of DR_1
proof_stages.summary: The stronger structural theorem says that DR_1(mu) is cut out by linear tropical incidence relations alone, and in the matroidal case its cell structure and dimensions are described by the elementary quotient lattice.

proof_stages.label: Adjoints and the point-incidence property
proof_stages.summary: Adjoints of matroids and valuated matroids are characterized by Bergman fans lying inside DR_1, and the existence of an adjoint gives stable-intersection control proving the tropical analogue of putting d−1 points in a codimension-one subspace.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions to completing partial valuated flags
proof_stages.summary: For the flag-completion problem, the speaker reports that a naive stronger version with prescribed underlying matroid flag is false, using rigidity and dimension-count obstructions coming from finite projective planes.

proof_stages.label: Modularity failure and final Lorentzian convex-hull theorem
proof_stages.summary: The intersection problem is reframed as submodularity/modularity of quotient posets, shown to fail generally by a matroid counterexample, but a rank-three positive result for tropical lines in a tropical plane yields a Lorentzian convex-hull theorem via degree-two reduction and an interlacing lemma.

examples.name: Classical Grassmannian inclusion
examples.note: The inclusion of Grassmannians for codimension-one subspaces of a fixed vector space explains by analogy why quadratic Plücker equations can become linear under incidence constraints.
examples.engagement_reason: The classical Grassmannian inclusion is traced through induced wedge maps and a commutative diagram to explain why the smaller Grassmannian sits linearly inside the larger one.

passes.reason: The codimension-one Grassmannian is a homogeneous projective-space endpoint whose linear inclusion is a classical analogue.
